coredata.dc:title: Achieving highly corrosion resistant and stable deep cycling zinc metal anode constructed by coal-based carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yingying
Wang, Ting
Chen, Xinyue
Li, Hao
Sun, Jixuan
Meng, Bohua
Wufuer, Anaerguli
Liu, Penggao
coredata.dc:description: The deterioration of zinc (Zn) anodes presents a significant challenge, often limiting the operational lifespan and efficiency of batteries. Addressing this issue is critical for advancing Zn-ion battery technology. This research proposes an innovative solution by employing coal-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance zinc anodes, mitigating corrosion and improving their performance in deep cycling applications. The CQDs facilitate uniform Zn2 + deposition through abundant zincophilic functional groups (e.g., -COO and -OH) and form a protective layer on the zinc surface to regulate Zn2+ desolvation and nucleation behavior. This ensures improved reversibility and stability of the Zn anode. Additionally, the hydrophilic and sulfate-ion-repelling (SO4 2--phobic) properties of the CQDs effectively suppress corrosion reactions at the zinc interface. As a result, the Zn@CQDs electrode demonstrates remarkable operational durability, maintaining stable performance for over 1700 hours at 1 mA cm−2 and over 500 hours at 5 mA cm−2. Furthermore, under a depth-of-discharge of 20 mAh cm−2, it sustains charge-discharge cycling for 800 hours, achieving an impressive coulombic efficiency of 99.8 %. Notably, the Zn@CQDs-2|| V2O5 full battery exhibits outstanding cyclability, achieving a cycle life of 1600 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, compared to 480 cycles for a conventional Zn||V2O5 full battery. It also delivers exceptional rate performance. These findings underline the potential of coal-based CQDs as a transformative material for significantly enhancing the corrosion resistance and electrochemical stability of zinc metal anodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal-based Carbon Quantum Dots
Zinc Metal Anode
Corrosion Resistant
Suppressor dendrite
Deep-discharge cycling

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation process of the Zn@CQDs. (b) TEM and SAED images of CQDs. (c) FTIR spectra of CQDs. (d)XRD image of Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes. (e) XPS image of Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes. SEM images of (f) Zn electrode and (g) Zn@CQDs-2 electrode. SEM images of Zn and Zn@CQDs-2 electrodes soaked for (h) 7 day and (i) 14 day. Contact angle images (j) Zn electrode and (k) Zn@CQDs-2 electrode. XRD images of Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes soaked for (l) 7 day and (m) 14 day.

(a) Tafel plots at 1 mV s−1 of Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes. (b) LSV curves of Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes. (c) CA curves of symmetric cell with Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes at an overpotential of −150 mV. I-t test curves at a constant potential of 10 mV and impedance spectra before and following polarization of (d) Zn electrode and (e) Zn@CQDs-2 electrode. (f) Transference number of symmetric cell with Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes. Schematic illustration of zinc ion transport and Zn deposition behavior for the (g) Zn electrode (h) Zn@CQDs-2 electrode.

(a) Nyquist plots of symmetric cells with the Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes. (b) Galvanostatic plating curves of the Zn and Zn@CQDs-2 electrodes at 1 mA cm−2. (c) XRD images of Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn electrodes after cycling. (d) CV curves of Zn//Cu half cells with the Zn and Zn@CQDs-2 at 1 mV s−1. (e) CE curves of Zn//Cu half cells with the Zn, Zn@CQDs-1, Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn@CQDs-3 electrodes. (f) Rate performances of symmetric cells with Zn, Zn@CQDs-1, Zn@CQDs-2 and Zn@CQDs-3 electrodes. Cycling performances of different symmetric cells at (g) 1 mA cm−2, 1 mAh cm−2 and (h) 5 mA cm−2, 5 mAh cm−2. SEM images of (i) Zn and (j) Zn@CQDs-2 electrodes after cycling.

(a) Schematic illustration of Zn//V2O5 full cell device. (b) CV curves of Zn//V2O5 and Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 cells at 1 mV s−1. (c) CV curves of Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 cells at different sweep speeds. (d) AC impedance of Zn//V2O5 and Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 cells. (e) Rate performances of Zn/V2O5 and Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 cells. (f) GCD curves of Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 cell. (g) Long-cycle performance and CE of Zn//V2O5 and Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 cells at 1 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Achieving highly corrosion resistant and stable deep cycling zinc metal anode constructed by coal-based carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, Xinjiang 830017, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon based Energy Resources; College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon based Energy Resources, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, Xinjiang 830017, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon based Energy Resources, Xinjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon based Energy Resources; College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The deterioration of zinc (Zn) anodes presents a significant challenge, often limiting the operational lifespan and efficiency of batteries. Addressing this issue is critical for advancing Zn-ion battery technology. This research proposes an innovative solution by employing coal-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance zinc anodes, mitigating corrosion and improving their performance in deep cycling applications. The CQDs facilitate uniform Zn2 + deposition through abundant zincophilic functional groups (e.g., -COO and -OH) and form a protective layer on the zinc surface to regulate Zn2+ desolvation and nucleation behavior. This ensures improved reversibility and stability of the Zn anode. Additionally, the hydrophilic and sulfate-ion-repelling (SO4 2--phobic) properties of the CQDs effectively suppress corrosion reactions at the zinc interface. As a result, the Zn@CQDs electrode demonstrates remarkable operational durability, maintaining stable performance for over 1700 hours at 1 mA cm−2 and over 500 hours at 5 mA cm−2. Furthermore, under a depth-of-discharge of 20 mAh cm−2, it sustains charge-discharge cycling for 800 hours, achieving an impressive coulombic efficiency of 99.8 %. Notably, the Zn@CQDs-2|| V2O5 full battery exhibits outstanding cyclability, achieving a cycle life of 1600 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, compared to 480 cycles for a conventional Zn||V2O5 full battery. It also delivers exceptional rate performance. These findings underline the potential of coal-based CQDs as a transformative material for significantly enhancing the corrosion resistance and electrochemical stability of zinc metal anodes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In this study, an artificial coating was designed to stabilize the zinc anode by loading coal-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the zinc anode to form a protective layer on the zinc surface. The solvation and nucleation behaviors of Zn2+ were modulated by the zinc-friendly functional groups of CQDs (e.g., -COO and -OH) to promote uniform Zn2+ deposition. Meanwhile, the hydrophilic and electrostatic effects of CQDs can effectively inhibit the corrosion reaction at the zinc interface. Therefore, coal-based CQDs can significantly improve the corrosion resistance and electrochemical stability of zinc metal anode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The industrial rapid development undoubtedly enhances our life, but it comes with problems such as excessive energy consumption and severe environmental pollution, which drives the global transition to clean energy source, and the volatile of the clean energy source hinder their widespread application [1–3]. Energy storage systems, especially aqueous zinc-ion energy storage systems, have become a promising choice for large-scale applications based on high safety, exceptional energy density, low cost, and environmental compatibility. Zinc anode represents compelling benefits such as abundant availability, low toxicity, stable chemical properties and low redox potential, making them an attractive area of research focus [4–11].

Despite these advantages, practical applications of zinc anodes are hindered by critical challenges. The uneven distribution of ions leads to dendrite growth on the anode surface, while the interface between the zinc metal and the electrolyte faces complications such as hydrogen evolution, corrosion, and passivation. These issues intertwine, creating a self-reinforcing vicious cycle, ultimately resulting in low coulombic efficiency, reduced cycle life, and rapid capacity degradation. Such limitations substantially restrict the development and practical implementation of aqueous zinc-ion batteries [12–16]. To address the challenge of interface stability in zinc anodes for practical applications, considerable efforts have been focused on developing efficient and durable zinc anodes [17,18]. Researchers have explored diverse modification strategies from multiple perspectives, including the construction of protective layers for zinc anodes, the design of three-dimensional structures, separator modifications, and the incorporation of electrolyte additives [19–24]. Among these approaches, the development of surface coatings for zinc anodes has emerged as a particularly straightforward and effective method to achieve highly reversible and high-utilization zinc anodes. By regulating the zinc ion deposition process through zinc-philic sites within the interface layer, a smooth and uniform deposition layer can be achieved. Moreover, the interface coating acts as a spatial barrier between the electrolyte and the zinc anode, significantly reducing surface side reactions such as hydrogen evolution and corrosion. Notably, the construction of the interface layer offers considerable versatility in material selection and is accompanied by a relatively simple industrial preparation process [25–28]. Common coating materials include carbon-based substances [29,30], metals [31,32], organic polymers [33,34], and composites [35,36]. Among these, carbon-based materials are particularly well-suited for creating modified coatings on zinc anodes, owing to their inherent advantages such as high mechanical strength, excellent conductivity, low cost, porous structure, and environmental sustainability. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a type of carbon nanomaterial, are widely utilized in medical, catalytic, and energy-related fields due to their numerous beneficial properties [37,38]. These include simple preparation processes, abundant raw material availability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, water solubility, and ease of functionalization. The surface of CQDs contains abundant functional groups that exhibit strong zinc-philicity, which facilitates zinc-ion migration and ensures a more uniform distribution of the electric field. Additionally, the modified layer binds with water molecules from the electrolyte while repelling SO4²⁻ ions, thereby mitigating side reactions such as hydrogen evolution and corrosion that arise from the zinc anode's interaction with the electrolyte [39,40].

In this study, CQD-assisted binders were employed as an interfacial modification layer for zinc anodes, referred to as Zn@CQDs. The modified zinc anodes create uniform ion channels to facilitate ion transport and ensure a homogeneous distribution of the surface electric field, effectively suppressing dendrite growth. Additionally, Zn@CQDs exploit the charge properties of CQDs to repel SO4²⁻ ions, thereby reducing the interaction between zinc metal and water and minimizing side reactions at the zinc anode interface. Symmetrical cells featuring CQD-modified zinc anodes exhibit remarkable stability for over 1600 hours at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2, maintaining excellent cycling performance even at higher current densities of 5 mA cm−2 and 5 mAh cm−2. Notably, under deep charge-discharge conditions of 1 mA cm−2 and 20 mAh cm−2, Zn@CQDs-2//Zn@CQDs-2 demonstrate stable cycling for 800 hours, significantly improving the electrochemical stability of the zinc anode.

As illustrated in Fig. 1a, traditional pure zinc anode encounter significant challenges during the charge and discharge cycles. These include uncontrolled dendrite growth, by-product accumulation, and gas evolution at the electrode interface, all of which severely compromise their electrochemical performance. To overcome these limitations, a CQDs-modified electrode interface was introduced to improve the performance of zinc anodes. The abundance of functional groups in CQDs aids in redistributing zinc ions, promoting their rapid migration, inducing uniform deposition, and effectively suppressing dendrite formation. Furthermore, these functional groups assist in adsorbing active water, thereby mitigating the hydrogen evolution reaction and reducing corrosion. The morphological properties of the CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which confirmed their spherical shape, an amorphous surface structure, and particle sizes ranging from 1 to 3 nm with an average size of 1.8 nm (Fig. 1b, Figure S1). These uniformly small CQDs facilitate the development of a consistent interfacial protective layer. This observation corresponds to the broad hump around 23° detected in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Figure S2). The chemical composition of CQDs were further analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As illustrated in Figure S3, XPS analysis indicates that CQDs are primarily composed of carbon (C) and oxygen (O). Combined with infrared spectral data, it is evident that the CQD surfaces are enriched with oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. 1c). These groups facilitate interactions with zinc ions, promoting uniform transport and deposition on the electrode's surface. Additionally, they form hydrogen bonds with active water molecules in the aqueous electrolyte, reducing direct contact between zinc and water at the interface, thereby effectively suppressing corrosion reactions [41,42]. The Zeta potential of CQDs (Figure S4) reveals that the quantum dots possess a negatively charged surface. This property attracts positively charged zinc ions while repelling similarly charged sulfate ions, minimizing side reactions caused by corrosion and further enhancing electrochemical performance [43]. The procedure for modifying pure zinc sheets with CQDs is detailed in the supplementary information. As shown in Fig. 1d, the XRD analysis of the CQD-modified zinc sheet displays a more pronounced 002 crystal plane, indicating that zinc ions preferentially deposit at a slight angle. This facilitates the formation of a deposition morphology parallel to the substrate, an advantageous feature for zinc anodes [44]. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was employed to examine the electrode's surface structure, as depicted in Figs. 1f and 1g. While the SEM image of the unmodified zinc sheet reveals prominent scratches, the surface of the CQD-modified zinc anode appears dense and smooth due to the protective coating formed by CQDs. Contact angle measurements, presented in Figs. 1h and 1i, indicate that the contact angle of the modified zinc sheet decreases by 19.7°. This reduction can be attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the oxygen-containing functional groups, which facilitate the rapid transport and desolvation of zinc ions. To assess corrosion resistance, the modified and unmodified zinc sheets were immersed in a zinc sulfate electrolyte for 7 and 14 days. As observed in Figs. 1j and 1k, after 7 days, disorderly hexagonal by-products form on the surface of the pure zinc sheet. These by-products obstruct the deposition and stripping processes of the zinc anode, leading to decreased coulombic efficiency. Prolonged immersion exacerbates this accumulation on the pure zinc sheet. In contrast, the CQD-coated electrode surface shows no significant by-product formation, consistent with the XRD results in Figs. 1l and 1m. Immersion tests conclusively demonstrate that the CQD coating enhances the corrosion resistance of the zinc anode's surface. As illustrated in the electrode cross-section (Figure S5), the CQD coating adheres closely to the zinc substrate, enabling effective zinc ion transport and uniform deposition.

The electrochemical properties of the modified zinc sheet were comprehensively analyzed. The corrosion resistance of Zn@CQDs-2 was evaluated using Tafel curves and LSV tests, as depicted in Fig. 2a. Remarkably, the modified zinc sheet exhibited a significantly lower corrosion current density (1.22 mA cm⁻²) compared to the unmodified zinc sheet (2.48 mA cm⁻²), demonstrating its effectiveness in suppressing corrosion reactions on the zinc anode. Additionally, the LSV diagram (Fig. 2b) revealed that the modified zinc sheet possesses a higher hydrogen evolution overpotential. This property plays a critical role in mitigating the hydrogen evolution reaction on the zinc anode’s surface, ultimately enhancing the cycle life of the battery. The diffusion and deposition behavior of zinc ions on the surface of the modified zinc sheets were further investigated using a chronoamperometry (CA) test. As presented in Fig. 2c, zinc ions displayed random two-dimensional diffusion at a constant overpotential of −150 mV. For Zn//Zn symmetric batteries, the current density increased beyond 100 seconds, indicating that ions diffused across the surface in two dimensions, searching for optimal charge transfer sites. The zinc ions tended to aggregate and grow as dendrites to minimize surface energy and exposed area. In contrast, Zn@CQDs-2//Zn@CQDs-2 symmetric batteries exhibited stable current profiles, after a short 2D plane diffusion and nucleation of 50 seconds exhibiting a stable 3D diffusion process. This behavior can be attributed to the advantageous role of CQDs in promoting the uniform migration, nucleation, and smooth growth of zinc ions, thereby enabling the formation of more consistent zinc anodes. Adsorption and desolvation of Zn(H2O)6 2+ occurs on the EDL structure close to the surface, and the reaction kinetics depends largely on the EDL value. In Figure S6, The EDL value of Zn@CQDs-2 is 3.05 mF cm−2, which is much higher than that of bare Zn (1.23 mF cm−2), this indicates that the transport kinetics of Zn ions on the anode surface was improved after the modification of the zincophilic CQDs. To better understand the regulatory effect of CQDs on zinc ion deposition, Zn//Zn symmetric batteries were employed for I-t tests and to evaluate the migration numbers of zinc ions from different zinc anodes (Figs. 2d and 2e). The migration number for Zn@CQDs-2 (0.72) was notably higher than that of pure zinc (0.36), as shown in Fig. 2f. This improvement is attributed to the presence of CQDs, which effectively minimize the gradient and polarization at the electrode/electrolyte interface, thereby enhancing the migration efficiency of zinc ions. Figs. 2g and 2h further illustrate the migration and deposition patterns of zinc ions on both pure and modified zinc sheets, providing further evidence of the superior performance of the modified zinc anodes.

As demonstrated in Fig. 3a, the impedance of the quantum dot-modified zinc sheet is noticeably lower than that of the pure zinc sheet. This indicates that quantum dots significantly enhance electron transport at the electrode interface, thereby improving the electrochemical performance of the battery. Similarly, Fig. 3b reveals that symmetrical cells assembled with modified zinc sheets exhibit a reduced nucleation overpotential. This reduction lowers the energy barrier for zinc ion deposition, promoting uniform zinc ion deposition. Phase analysis of symmetrical batteries assembled with both types of zinc sheets after 50 cycles, as shown in Fig. 3c, indicates that the modified zinc sheet experiences minimal corrosion compared to the pure zinc sheet. Fig. 3d depicts the CV curves of Zn@CQDs-2//Cu and Zn//Cu half-cells, which exhibit similar positions and shapes. The potential difference between points A and B reflects the uniform deposition tendencies of zinc ions [45]. Notably, due to CQD modification, the Zn@CQDs-2 demonstrates a 29 mV reduction in potential difference compared to the pure zinc sheet, highlighting enhanced uniformity in zinc deposition. Coulombic efficiency (CE) is a critical parameter for evaluating the reversibility of the zinc plating and stripping process and determining the practicality of aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) [46]. To assess this, Zn//Cu asymmetrical batteries were tested at a current density of 1 mA cm⁻² with a capacity density of 1 mAh cm⁻². As seen in Fig. 3e, the Coulombic efficiency of Zn@CQDs-2//Cu asymmetrical batteries remained consistently high, achieving a retention rate of 99.14 % over 600 cycles. In contrast, the CE of Zn//Cu, Zn@CQDs-1//Cu, and Zn@CQDs-3//Cu asymmetrical cells exhibited considerable fluctuations. Fig. 3f illustrates that appropriately modified quantum dots provide optimal rate capability. The cyclic performance of symmetrical cells, employing different types of zinc anodes under varying current densities, was examined to assess the impact of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on zinc anode stability [47]. Fig. 3g shows that under the conditions of 1 mA cm⁻² and 1 mAh cm⁻², the zinc sheet modified with Zn@CQDs-2 demonstrated stable cycling for over 1600 hours, while the unmodified Zn//Zn cells endured approximately 200 cycles under identical conditions. Furthermore, the cycling performance of symmetrical batteries using zinc sheets modified with varying volumes of quantum dot mixtures was evaluated. A smaller amount of CQDs-1 did not significantly improve cycle performance, while excessive CQDs-3 modifications adversely affected battery cycle life, likely due to agglomeration issues that hinder zinc ion migration. When the current density was increased to 5 mA cm⁻² and 5 mAh cm⁻², Zn@CQDs-2//Zn@CQDs-2 maintained stable cycling for over 400 hours, whereas pure zinc sheets lasted only about 100 hours under comparable conditions. Additionally, comprehensive charge-discharge tests were conducted. Under conditions of 1 mA cm⁻² and 20 mAh cm⁻², the Zn@CQDs-2 symmetric battery exhibited stable cycling for 800 hours (Fig. 3h). This finding underscores that modifying the zinc cathode surface with quantum dots can considerably improve the utilization rate of the zinc anode and extend the battery's cycle life. Finally, SEM images of various zinc anodes after 50 cycles were analyzed (Figs. 3i and 3j). The results revealed that symmetric batteries assembled with pure zinc sheets displayed large acicular products on their surfaces, obstructing uniform zinc ion deposition. In contrast, modified zinc sheets maintained relatively flat surfaces with more uniform ion deposition, further demonstrating the efficacy of quantum dot modification.Moreover, Figure S7 shows the comparison graphs on the cycling performance of different modified materials of Zn anodes and Zn@CQDs-2 electrode at different current densities.

To further validate the practical feasibility of the Zn@CQDs negative electrode, a Zn//V₂O₅ full battery ( Fig. 4a) and a zinc-ion hybrid capacitor (Figure S8) were assembled. Fig. 4b and S9 depict the CV curves of the full battery and hybrid capacitor utilizing different zinc sheets. The CV curves display two pairs of REDOX peaks with similar positions and shapes, signifying that the addition of CQDs does not induce any additional REDOX reactions. An expansion in the area under the curves indicates that the incorporation of quantum dots enhances the electrochemical performance of these devices. Furthermore, Fig. 4c and S10 illustrate the CV profiles of Zn@CQDs//V₂O₅ and Zn@CQDs//AC at various scanning rates, demonstrating favorable electrochemical kinetics. As seen in Fig. 4d and S11, batteries incorporating the Zn@CQDs-2 anode exhibit reduced impedance compared to those using the pristine Zn anode, thereby enhancing their applicability in practical scenarios. Additionally, Figs. 4e and 4f show the rate performance of the Zn@CQDs//V2O5 battery, while Figure S12 presents the rate capabilities of the zinc-ion hybrid capacitor. These results clearly highlight that the modified zinc-based devices deliver superior rate performance. At a current density of 1 A g⁻¹ , the cyclic performance analysis in Fig. 4g reveals that the pristine Zn//V₂O₅ battery fails after approximately 400 cycles, while the Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 battery achieves operational stability for over 1500 cycles. In addition, the self-discharge performance of Zn//V2O5 and Zn@CQDs-2//V2O5 was investigated. As shown in Figure. S13, the battery with Zn@CQDs-2 electrodes retained 90.5 % of its initial capacity, much higher than the 78.8 % of bare Zn. The addition of carbon quantum dots can help full batteries significantly suppress self-discharge behavior and enhance the energy storage stability of the batteries. Figure S14 further illustrates the cycling performance and coulombic efficiency of the Zn@CQDs-2//AC capacitor, along with a commendable capacity retention rate. These findings underscore that the incorporation of CQDs significantly enhances the reversibility and durability of the batteries, establishing their superior performance and reliability for practical applications.

The zinc anode, coated with carbon quantum dots (CQDs), demonstrates remarkable advantages in electrochemical performance. In particular, the negative electrode exhibits significantly enhanced cyclic stability and coulombic efficiency, along with notable improvements in charge-discharge capabilities and energy density. These findings underscore the effectiveness of the CQD protective coating in enhancing the electrochemical properties of zinc metal anodes, thereby offering strong support for their application in energy storage and conversion systems. This study not only presents an innovative strategy to improve the stability and electrochemical performance of zinc metal anodes but also provides valuable insights into the potential of coal-based CQDs in electrochemistry. With continued advancements in research and technology, coal-based CQDs are expected to showcase their distinctive advantages and wide-ranging applications across various fields.

Anaerguli Wufuer: Writing – review & editing. Bohua Meng: Investigation. Jixuan Sun: Investigation. Hao Li: Investigation. Xinyue Chen: Data curation. Ting Wang: Data curation. Yingying Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Penggao Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. All authors have seen and approved the final version of the manuscript being submitted. We warrant that the article is the authors' original work hasn't received prior publication and isn't under consideration for publication elsewhere. There is no financial/personal interest or belief, the potential conflicts don't exist.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22208273), and Tianchi Talent Plan of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.137316.
